body.p:
XML data can become very complex. Since their input is often not restricted by any user interface measures or other regulations, they can in principle be modified with any text editor and are usually sent/processed through many stages, it is advisable to check their formal and content correctness.
UWE test footnote (10/40, callout J): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.

For this purpose, the good machine readability of XML data is exploited by validation engines. There are many different schema types that can be validated against. The so-called schema is then a set of rules that is compared during the tree traversal.
It all probably started with the Document Type Definition (DTD Wikipedia page on the topic of DTD)SchemasDTD which can be applied to both XML data and the predecessor SGML.
The lack of ability to check semantic relationships using DTD probably led to XML Schema XML Schema.

XML SchemaSchemasXML Schema is consistently modeled using XML and has the same good property regarding machine readability as XML itself. Thus, during a tree traversal, even complex rules can be machine-checked when comparing data structure and validation rule.

Perhaps, however, this very advantage, namely machine readability, has crystallized as a disadvantage over time. Although there are some very good visual modeling tools that allow the rules to be graphically modeled as a tree, as soon as more complex relationships need to be modeled, you are somewhat trapped with these tools and you wish again for the flexibility of a text editor.

li.p: A tool that looks like it is from another century: The Near & Far Designer Last resource on the web for the Near & Far Designer DTD modelling tool
TreeVision Website of the TreeVision tool by Ovidius GmbH by Ovidius GmbH in Berlin.ToolsTreeVision
Visual Schema Editor Graphical component of the oXygen XML Editor for schemas as part of the oXygen XML Editor.ToolsVisual Schema Editor

fig.title: Display of a DTD structure in the Near&Far Designer
fig.desc: A DTD is displayed in the Near&Far Designer as an expandable tree structure. This way, even very complex DTDs with 1000 elements can be efficiently examined. Attributes can be added or changed via an input dialog. Creating new branches (= adding elements) can also be done graphically. However, it is advisable to sketch at least the basic framework of the DTD with a text editor.

body.p: Similar to programming environments, what ultimately matters is how quickly you can find and adapt/extend something in extensive source code. Here, plain text remains unbeatable.

Therefore, it is not surprising that lightweight validation formats are (re-)establishing themselves that can be nicely edited with a text editor, such as RelaxNGSchemasRelaxNG . RelaxNG has existed since 2002 but has been enjoying increasing popularity recently.

The compact form of RelaxNG rules looks a bit like JSON - which might be interesting for web developers. Additionally, the underlying logic of the Backus-Naur Form Wikipedia page on the Backus-Naur Form is presented relatively clearly, which facilitates rule definition.
To get a first impression of the syntax, I took the first example from the RelaxNG Tutorial RelaxNG Tutorial:
Consider a simple XML representation of an email address book:
The DTD would be as follows:
body.pre: <!DOCTYPE addressBook [ <!ELEMENT addressBook (card*)> <!ELEMENT card (name, email)> <!ELEMENT name (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT email (#PCDATA)> ]>
body.p: A RELAX NG pattern for this could be written as follows:
In compact form, this example would look like this:
There is also a tutorial for the compact form Tutorial for the RelaxNG compact form.

note.p: Schema files written in compact form typically have the file extension .rnc. Files in the long form have the extension .rng.

body.p: With RelaxNG you can model schema grammars almost as elegantly as with the Backus-Naur Normal Form - BNF Wikipedia page on BNF - as you learned in computer science class.

section.title: Exclusion with RNC
section.p: The beauty of RelaxNG is the fact that you can do things with it that are not easily done with other schema languages. For example, you can create an incomplete schema that only checks very specific parts of the XML instance.
Let us consider the following example:ConceptsExclusion with RelaxNG

We know how the elements abrechnung and zahlung are structured and can model them completely. The unknown quantity, however, is the beleg element. This comes from an external source, and we only know that the fields datum, empfaenger and aussteller must be present within it.

Between these elements there is at least one, but possibly more, unknown elements. So that we can validate XML instances structured according to this schema, an element unbehandeltesElement is modeled, which effectively represents a placeholder. This explicitly excludes the mandatory fields datum, empfaenger and aussteller so as not to falsify their validation by the schema.

section.title: Relaxtron
section.p: Particularly promising is the ability to embed additional Schematron rulesSchematron in a RelaxNG schema - Relaxtron Relaxtron (xml.com). This allows you to ensure, for example, that certain constraints regarding the element structure are maintained. This would not be possible with a schema language alone. Let us consider the following piece of RelaxNG in compact form:
Here it is ensured that an unordered list list is not nested deeper - but only within a procedure.
The oXygen Editor can convert the RelaxNG compact form into the XML form and visualize it nicely. This then looks like this:

fig.title: Display of a RelaxNG schema in the oXygen Editor
fig.desc: In the oXygen Editor there is a full model view and a logical model view for RelaxNG schemas that also display embedded Schematron nodes. By clicking on a symbol, you navigate to the corresponding source code section.

section.title: Specifying the Schema in the XML Instance
section.p: As already described in the chapter Application Areas, all additional instructions important for the XML processor that are not part of the data are embedded in the XML via a Processing Instruction XML ConstructsProcessing Instruction .
Multiple schemas can also be embedded via a Processing Instruction, so that validation across multiple engines is possible.
If you take over a legacy data set for which only a DTD is available, an XML Schema can also be added after the fact to check more complex semantic relationships.
For further validation, Schematron is available, cf. chapter Validation with Schematron . An example of how to specify multiple schemas can be found on the W3 Consortium page on the topic xml-model
To include a RelaxNG compact form, it suffices to specify this PI (Processing InstructionXML ConstructsProcessing Instruction ):
oXygen and all other editors support specifying the schema via PI, so you can validate in the editor without an explicit request.

section.title: oXygen Validation
section.p: The validation tools can be found in the oXygen Editor under Document > Validate > Validate With

fig.title: Validation dialog in oXygen
fig.desc: The validation options can be found in oXygen under the [[code:Document]] tab.

section.p: Schemas can also be converted with oXygen, Document > Schema > Generate/Convert Schema

fig.title: Schema conversion with oXygen
fig.desc: Conversion tools are also located in the [[code:Document]] tab

section.title: Jing and Trang
section.p: For RelaxNG there is the Jing Validator Jing. Notably, this tool is developed by James Clark James Clark (a veteran of the XML data world).

Not quite on topic, but: A tool by James ClarkXML GurusJames Clark that I have worked with several times is SP, an SGML systemToolsSP, with which you can convert SGML to XML. This functionality is otherwise rather rare to find …

messagepanel.typeofhazard: James Clark’s SP: SGML-to-XML conversion (tangential note on schemas).

section.p: To validate an XML instance with Jing, the following command line call suffices:

messagepanel.typeofhazard: Jing does not support the RelaxNG compact form!
messagepanel.howtoavoid: The compact form must first be converted to the normal form with Trang.

section.p: To convert the compact form of RelaxNG to the normal form, you issue this command on the command line:

section.title: Schema Single-Sourcing
section.p:
Single-SourcingSchemas
BuzzwordsSingle Source Publishing The single-sourcing concept does not stop at schemas either. From a single source in the RelaxNG compact form, alternative views in other schema languages can be generated, such as XML Schema (XSD) XML Schema.

fig.title: BPMN diagram for the single-sourcing concept with RelaxNG schemas
fig.desc: A BPMN diagram can nicely illustrate the single-sourcing concept in schema generation. From a single [[code:RNC]] source, the RelaxNG XML form, the Schematron rules, the XSD, and the XSLT of the Schematron rules are generated. These artifacts are used for validation in the editor and for validation in a transformer pipe.

section.p: A shell script that invokes the process could look like this, for example:
section.pre:
#!/bin/sh
echo "converting from RelaxNG compact form to RelaxNG XML form..." trang -I rnc -O rng test.rnc test.rng
echo "extracting embedded schematron rules from RelaxNG schema..." saxon -s:test.rng -o:test.sch -xsl:rng2sch.xsl
echo "converting RelaxNG schema to XML Schema..." trang -O xsd test.rng test.xsd
echo "converting schematron rules to XSLT..." saxon test.sch validation/schematron/iso_dsdl_include.xsl | \ saxon -s:- validation/schematron/iso_abstract_expand.xsl | \ saxon -s:- validation/schematron/iso_svrl_for_xslt2.xsl > test.xsl
echo "Done!"
section.p: An unpolished script for extracting the Schematron rulesSchematron from the RNC can be found on GitHub Raw script for extracting Schematron rules from an RNC compact form.
